en.extract:
Adelaide's warbler (Setophaga adelaidae), or reinita mariposera (in Puerto Rican Spanish) is a bird endemic to the archipelago of Puerto Rico belonging to the genus Setophaga of the family Parulidae (New World warblers). 


Taxonomy
The S. adelaidae complex was originally considered a single species, with three populations occurring in Barbuda, Puerto Rico and St. Lucia. Each of these populations were regarded as a subspecies, S.a. subita, S.a. adelaidae and S.a. delicata respectively. These subspecies were later elevated to species rank as the Barbuda warbler (Setophaga subita), the St. Lucia warbler (Setophaga delicata) and Adelaide's warbler.
In 2011, the American Ornithologists' Union reclassified the Parulidae, which resulted in D. adelaidae being transferred to the genus Setophaga.
The species is named after Maria Antoinette Adelaide Florentia del Carmen Swift Washburne (1829–1884), daughter of Robert Swift, the person who obtained the first specimen.


Description
Adelaide's warbler has gray upperparts with yellow underparts. The species has a yellow line above the eye and a white half-moon below it. Its average length is 12 cm (4.7 in) and its average weight is 7 g (0.25 oz).


Distribution and habitat
Adelaide's warbler occurs only in the main island of Puerto Rico and in the island municipality of Vieques. The species occurs mainly in dry forests in the southern region of Puerto Rico such as the Guánica State Forest, with some occurrences in the northern moist forests and the central mountain range, the Cordillera Central.


Diet and ecology
Adelaide's warbler is an insectivore which gleans insects from the mid-top areas of the forest. It is also known to eat, although very rarely, spiders and small amphibians such as coquís. The species usually travels in mixed flocks which commonly include Puerto Rican todies, vireos and other New World warblers. 


Reproduction
Adelaide's warblers build nests at heights of 1 to 7 m in which the female deposits anywhere from 2 to 4 white eggs. The eggshells usually have small brown spots.


See also

El Toro Wilderness


References


Further reading
"Adelaide's Warbler – Dendroica Adelaidae". Oiseaux.net. Archived from the original on 7 March 2006. Retrieved April 7, 2006.
(in Spanish) Sociedad Ortinológica Puertorriqueña – Reinita Mariposera
Oberle, Mark (2003). Las aves de Puerto Rico en fotografías (in Spanish). Editorial Humanitas. ISBN 0-9650104-2-2.

'''Adelaide's warbler''' ('''''Setophaga adelaidae'''''), or '''reinita mariposera''' (in [[Puerto Rican Spanish]]) is a [[bird]] [[endemic (ecology)|endemic]] to the [[archipelago]] of [[Puerto Rico]] belonging to the genus ''Setophaga'' of the family [[New World warbler|Parulidae]] (New World warblers). 

== Taxonomy ==
The ''S. adelaidae'' complex was originally considered a single species, with three populations occurring in [[Barbuda]], Puerto Rico and [[St. Lucia]]. Each of these populations were regarded as a subspecies, ''S.a. subita'', ''S.a. adelaidae'' and ''S.a. delicata'' respectively. These subspecies were later elevated to species rank as the [[Barbuda warbler]] (''Setophaga subita''), the [[St. Lucia warbler]] (''Setophaga delicata'') and Adelaide's warbler.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Staicer, Cynthia A. |date=October 1996 |title=Acoustical features of song categories of the Adelaide's Warbler (''Dendroica adelaidae'') |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v113n04/p0771-p0783.pdf |journal=The Auk |volume=113 |issue=4 |pages=771–783 |doi=10.2307/4088856 |jstor=4088856}}</ref>

In 2011, the American Ornithologists' Union reclassified the Parulidae, which resulted in ''D. adelaidae'' being transferred to the genus ''Setophaga.''<ref>{{cite journal |author=Chesser, R. Terry |author2=Richard C. Banks |author3=F. Keith Barker |author4=Carla Cicero |author5=Jon L. Dunn |author6=Andrew W. Kratter |author7=Irby J. Lovette |author8=Pamela C. Rasmussen |author9=J. V. Remsen |author10=James D. Rising |author11=Douglas F. Stotz |author12=Kevin Winker |year=2011 |title=Fifty-second supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union Check-List of North American Birds |url=http://www.aou.org/checklist/north/suppl/52.php |journal=Auk |volume=128 |issue=3 |pages=600–613 |doi=10.1525/auk.2011.128.3.600 |s2cid=13691956 |doi-access=free|bibcode=2011Auk...128..600C }}</ref>

The species is named after Maria Antoinette Adelaide Florentia del Carmen Swift Washburne (1829–1884), daughter of Robert Swift, the person who obtained the first specimen.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Beolens |first1=Bo |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=g_3QDwAAQBAJ |title=The Eponym Dictionary of Birds |last2=Watkins |first2=Michael |last3=Grayson |first3=Michael |date=2020-03-19 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4729-8269-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Sellers |first=Edwin Jaquett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ac4PAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA58 |title=Genealogy of the Kollock Family of Sussex County, Delaware, 1657-1897 |date=1897 |publisher=Press of J.B. Lippincott Company |language=en}}</ref>

==Description==
Adelaide's warbler has gray upperparts with yellow underparts. The species has a yellow line above the eye and a white half-moon below it. Its average length is {{Convert|12|cm|in|abbr=on}} and its average weight is {{Convert|7|g|oz|abbr=on}}.

== Distribution and habitat ==
Adelaide's warbler occurs only in the main island of Puerto Rico and in the island municipality of [[Vieques, Puerto Rico|Vieques]]. The species occurs mainly in [[Puerto Rican dry forests|dry forests]] in the southern region of Puerto Rico such as the [[Guánica State Forest]], with some occurrences in the northern [[Puerto Rican moist forests|moist forests]] and the central mountain range, the [[Cordillera Central (Puerto Rico)|Cordillera Central]].

== Diet and ecology ==
Adelaide's warbler is an [[insectivore]] which [[Gleaning (birds)|gleans]] insects from the mid-top areas of the forest. It is also known to eat, although very rarely, [[spider]]s and small [[amphibian]]s such as [[coquí]]s. The species usually travels in [[mixed-species feeding flock|mixed flocks]] which commonly include [[Puerto Rican tody|Puerto Rican todies]], [[vireo]]s and other [[New World warbler]]s. 

== Reproduction ==
Adelaide's warblers build nests at heights of 1 to 7 m in which the female deposits anywhere from 2 to 4 white eggs. The eggshells usually have small brown spots.
